title: Green oak peg rack
subtitle: Wall-mounted peg rack with riven-oak rail and carved pegs
excerpt: A wall-mounted peg rack: a riven green-oak back rail with four round pegs bored into it. The pegs are shaped with a drawknife and knife; they are fitted green and shrink tight as they dry. No glue needed.
sourceNotes: Edlin, H.L., Woodland Crafts in Britain (1949) on green-wood peg and treen fitting. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III on riven-wood construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled timber, still high in moisture. Pegs fitted green shrink tight into their bored holes as they dry.
Split along the grain with a froe. Riven oak is far stronger than sawn oak for a structural rail.
A wedge-shaped blade with a vertical handle, driven into the end-grain of a log to split it along the grain.
A short length of riven timber ready to be shaped.
A two-handled blade pulled toward the body along the workpiece, used at the shaving horse to shape the peg blanks.
A foot-pedalled bench that clamps the workpiece for drawknife work.
A knife cut driven away from the body, used for the final shaping of the peg tips.

content.text: A
green-wood
peg rack uses shrinkage to its advantage: the
riven
oak pegs are fitted green, and as they dry, the fibres shrink around the bored hole and lock tight without glue. The back rail is also riven oak;
riven
oak splits along the medullary rays and produces a very strong flat face. The
froe
and beetle make the initial split; the
drawknife
refines the rail and peg shapes on the
shaving horse
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
One green oak
billet

for the rail: 50 cm long, 8 cm wide, 3 cm thick after riving and dressing. Four green oak peg blanks: each 15 cm long, 3.5 cm diameter round section. Oak from a freshly felled or recently coppiced tree; air-dried oak is too hard for the drawknife.
Tools

A froe and beetle for the initial rive. A drawknife and shaving horse for dressing the rail face and shaping the peg blanks. A brace and bit (25 mm bit) for the peg holes. A sloyd knife for the peg tips. A leather strop and raw linseed oil.
Method
Rive the rail blank from the green oak log. Drive the froe into the end-grain with the beetle; lever the handle to open the split. Work the froe down the length to separate the rail section.
Dress the rail face flat on the shaving horse with the drawknife. Work in long strokes from one end to the other. Check the face with a straight-edge; a 2 mm hollow along the length is acceptable.
Shape the four peg blanks on the shaving horse. Each peg has a 12 cm working section (30 mm diameter, round) and a 3 cm shoulder section (32 mm diameter) where it enters the rail.
Push cuts
on the sloyd knife round the peg tip to a gentle dome.

Mark the four peg-hole positions on the rail face: 10 cm from each end, then two equally spaced between them. Bore the holes with the brace and 25 mm bit. Bore perpendicular to the face; use a try-square beside the brace to check the angle.

Drive each green peg into its hole with the beetle. The peg should enter with moderate resistance and sit with the shoulder flush to the rail face. If the fit is too loose, wrap the peg shoulder with a strip of birch bark before driving.
Leave the assembled rack for 4 weeks before hanging. The pegs will tighten as they dry.
Finishing
Apply raw linseed oil to the rail face and all peg surfaces. One coat is enough for interior use. Re-oil once a year.
Variations
Ash instead of oak: lighter and slightly more open grain. The shrinkage joint works with any strong hardwood.
Longer rail with six pegs for a hall rack: extend to 80 cm, six holes equally spaced.
Decorative peg tips: carve a small carved bead or taper on each peg tip with the sloyd knife before fitting.

items.cause: The bored hole diameter was too large for the peg shoulder diameter
items.fix: The peg shoulder should be 1 to 2 mm larger than the bit diameter before driving. The compression of the green wood during driving provides the initial grip; shrinkage adds the final grip.

items.cause: The grain deviated from the long axis of the log
items.fix: Sight along the log end-grain before starting. Rings that are off-centre or wavy indicate a deviated grain. Choose a straight-grained section.

items.cause: Peg tip thinned too much during shaping
items.fix: The peg tip dome should be no thinner than 15 mm across the flat face. Do not taper the peg below 20 mm diameter outside the shoulder section.
